text: This is a case of a thirty-four-year-old male with an acute cholecystitis. He had an ultrasound which showed a three centimeter stone impacted in the neck of the gallbladder.
He was in excruciating pain. You can see why here.
The surgeon observes pericholecystic edema and fluid around the gallbladder, with the infundibulum appearing adhered to surrounding structures.
You can see it'll be difficult to grab this thing here in a second. I'll show you.
The surgeon uses small hooks on the backside of the infundibulum to loosen it, improving visibility of the surrounding structures.
The surgeon administers additional indocyanine green (ICG) dye to confirm the location of the cystic artery, enhancing its visibility for precise identification.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct on the left and the cystic artery on the right, clearly distinguishing these structures in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to illuminate and confirm the positions of the cystic artery and cystic duct, achieving the critical view of safety. The triangle of Calot is clearly visible in the robotic surgical field.
The surgeon performs a quick check for the posterior artery, which remains slightly illuminated due to the ICG dye.
